content: Date 2026-01-07. Quick read of the speculative decoding paper — want a fast take on why it speeds up inference and what to try next. Core idea as I understand it: run a small, cheap draft model to propose a block of next tokens (k tokens), then use the large target model to verify/accept that block instead of generating each token autoregressively. That amortizes the large-model forward passes over k tokens and shifts work to the cheap model. Concrete numbers for my context: our heavy model is 70B (production), draft candidate we can run is 1B on the same node (~5% compute cost of the 70B forward). If I pick k=8 and the draft proposals are accepted a large fraction of the time, the paper claims 2–3× wall-clock speedups in similar setups. Why: fewer sequential expensive forwards, better GPU utilization and batching, and the draft lets you avoid repeated attention over long contexts on the big model. Key sensitivity points to watch: acceptance rate of the draft on our domain (October–December 2025 customer logs), how often the verifier forces rollbacks (rejections), and latency variance (we have p95 SLA). My initial plan: benchmark k in {2,4,8} with draft=1B (maybe 3B later), measure p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput and tokens per € (cost). Run on Jan 8–9 on 100k-token samples. Decision for now: prioritize k=4 and k=2 for latency-first tests. Thoughts?
Do you already have an estimate for the draft model acceptance rate on your Oct–Dec 2025 logs, or is that unknown?

Unknown — we haven't measured it. My gut guess from quick label runs is 0.6–0.8 block-accept rate for k=4 on in-domain prompts, but that's handwave. Dataset: ~1M tokens of real prompts/responses from Oct–Dec 2025, I can sample 100k tokens stratified by customer and prompt length. Experimental matrix I want: draft sizes {1B, 3B} × k {2,4,8} × metrics {p50,p95,p99,throughput,cost/1k tokens,reject-rate}. Also capture rollback overhead and memory spikes. Plan to run the 1B/draft k=2,4 first. Next steps: instrument acceptance-rate telemetry and compare to paper numbers.
Which single metric do you want to optimize first for choosing k: p95 latency or cost per 1k tokens?

p95 latency first — SLA requires predictable tails. Numbers: current production p95 per-token is ~180 ms (median 120 ms), cost ~€0.20 / 1k tokens. Target: p95 ≤ 150 ms and cost ≤ €0.15 / 1k if possible. If speculative gets p95 down to 120–140 ms with similar or lower cost, adopt. If it reduces median but leaves p95 spiky, we reject. So we need deterministic tails, not just median wins. I'll run k=2 and k=4 with 1B draft initially. Any recommendation on which k is more likely to reduce p95 deterministically?
If tail determinism matters, do you prefer conservative k (smaller k, fewer rollbacks) or aggressive k and accept occasional fallback spikes?

Conservative — start with k=2 and k=4 only. If k=4 shows stable p95 under 150 ms and <10% rollback-induced spikes, then try k=8. Baselines to record: per-token latency distribution, GPU utilization, and cost-per-1M tokens computed from actual runtime. Current baseline: 70B autoreg p95=180 ms, median=120 ms, cost=€0.20/1k. Plan benchmarks on Jan 8–9 with 100k tokens per config sampled from Oct–Dec 2025 logs. I also want to log worst-case prompts (longest context + rare tokens) separately. Anything else to add to the instrumentation?
Add per-block acceptance histogram and time-to-rollback distribution. Also track tail latency conditioned on rollbacks — OK?

Yes — add both. Also capture memory allotment per forward and position in context where rollbacks happen (early vs late in block). For cost math: I'll record GPU raw time and convert to € using our node rate €3.5/hr; that gives concrete cost/1M tokens. Quick sanity numbers: if draft forward is 5% of big, and k=4 with 70% block acceptance, I should expect ~2× reduction in big-model forwards and noticeable p95 drop — but real measurement will tell. Execution plan: Jan 8 start with k=2/1B and k=4/1B, 100k tokens each, then decide Jan 9 whether to run 3B drafts or k=8. I'll commit to reviewing results Jan 10. Anything else I should include in the run to make the Jan 10 decision unambiguous?
Include an adversarial synthetic set (worst-case prompts) and a small human-eval for quality regressions. Will 100k tokens + adversarial set be enough to decide on Jan 10?
